questions.prompt: According to a survey, 32 percent of people in a certain country regularly recycle. Mr. Giovanni, the mayor of a specific city in the country, wanted to know whether the residents in his city regularly recycle more than the national average. He selected a random sample of 50 residents, 28 of whom regularly recycle. Mr. Giovanni would like to use a one-sample 𝑧-test for a population proportion to test the hypotheses H0: 𝑝= 0.32 versus Ha: 𝑝> 0.32, where 𝑝 is the proportion of city residents who regularly recycle. In order to use this test, the sampling distribution of the sample proportion needs to be approximately normal. Which of the following is true for Mr. Giovanni's study and ensures that the sampling distribution of the sample proportion is approximately normal?

options.text: The distribution of the population of all residents who regularly recycle is approximately normal.
The sample size is 50, which is larger than 30 .
The sample size of 50 is less than 10% of the residents in the city.
Theexpectednumberofresidentswhoregularlyrecycleis16,andtheexpectednumberofresidentswhodonotregularlyrecycleis34,andbothnumbersareatleast10.
The observed number of residents who regularly recycle is 28, and the observed number of residents who do not regularly recycle is 22, and both numbers are at least 10.

questions.prompt: <span aria-label="Three months from now, all of the parents/guardians of elementary school students in a certain large school district will vote either yes or no on a new electronic tablet program. This program will allow every elementary school student within the school district to be issued a tablet. In a recent survey of a random sample of 300 parents/guardians of elementary school students, 146 out of 300 open paren 48.7 percentclose paren indicated they would vote yes in favor of such a program. Mr. Robinson, the technology expert at the school district, is in favor of the elementary students being issued a tablet. He develops a pamphlet showing the educational benefits of students having their own tablet. He did this to increase the proportion of parents/guardians voting yes. To investigate the effectiveness of the pamphlet, Mr. Robinson distributed the pamphlet to the same 300 parents/guardians from the original sample. After reading the pamphlet, each parent/guardian was asked again whether each elementary student should be issued a tablet. Parents/guardians could change their answer or keep the same answer from the original survey. The results of the parent/guardian replies are shown in the table. YES, after reading the pamphlet NO, after reading the pamphlet Total YES, before reading the pamphlet 110 36 146 NO, before reading the pamphlet 69 85 154 Total 179 121 300 open parenaclose paren Consider the data shown in the table. open pareniclose paren What proportion of the parents/guardians of elementary school students said YES after reading the pamphlet? open pareniiclose paren What proportion of parents/guardians of elementary school students said YES after reading the pamphlet given they said NO before reading the pamphlet? Mr. Robinson wants to conduct an inference test to investigate whether reading the pamphlet will result in a change in the level of support among all parents/guardians of elementary school students in the district for the tablet program when they vote in three months. open parenbclose paren Mr. Robinson is considering the use of a 𝑧-test for a difference of two population proportions for his inference test. open pareniclose paren What are the conditions of a 𝑧-test for a difference of two population proportions? open pareniiclose paren Explain why a 𝑧-test for a difference of two population proportions is not an appropriate test for Mr. Robinson to use. The table of parent/guardian replies is repeated here. YES, after reading the pamphlet NO, after reading the pamphlet Total YES, before reading the pamphlet 110 36 146 NO, before reading the pamphlet 69 85 154 Total 179 121 360 The district statistician explains to Mr. Robinson that the appropriate inference test to use is called McNemar's chi-square test. The hypotheses for McNemar's test are shown here. H0:𝑝1 = 𝑝2 H2:𝑝1 ≠𝑝2 Let 𝑝1 be the proportion of all parents / guardians of elementary school students in the district who would say NO before reading the pamphlet and would say YES after reading the pamphlet. Let 𝑝2 be the proportion of all parents/ guardians of elementary school students in the district who would say YES before reading the pamphlet and would say NO after reading the pamphlet. To apply McNemar's test in this study, consider only the 105 responses of the parents/ guardians whose opinions changed open paren i.e., 69 from NO to YES and 36 from YES to NOclose paren. McNemar's test statistic is given by 𝑥2 = open paren𝑏negative 𝑐close paren2 𝑏+ 𝑐 where 𝑏 represents the number of parents/guandians in the sample that said NO before reading the pamphlet and YES afier reading the pamphlet and 𝑐 represents the number of parents/guardians in the sample that said YES before reading the pamphlet and NO after reading the pamphlet: open parencclose paren open pareniclose paren Assume all conditions for McNemar's test have been met. Calculate the test statistic using the sample data from Mr Robinson's study. Show your work. open pareniiclose paren Assuming the null hypothesis is true, McNemar's test statistic, 𝜒2, is distributed approximately as a chi-square statistic with 1 degree of freedom.
